cards.front: What is the most important cell in wound healing?
cards.back: Macrophage - orchestrates healing by releasing cytokines and growth factors, recruiting fibroblasts, and performing phagocytosis

cards.front: What are the three phases of wound healing?
Which collagen type predominates in early wounds?
Which collagen type predominates in mature scars?
What is the maximum tensile strength a healed wound can achieve?
How long does it take to achieve maximum wound strength?
What is the difference between primary and secondary intention healing?
cards.back:
Primary: Wound edges approximated (surgical wound)
Secondary: Wound left open, heals by granulation (abscess cavity)

cards.front: What is tertiary intention (delayed primary closure)?
cards.back: Wound initially left open (contaminated), then closed primarily once clean (usually day 4-7)

cards.front: Why is Vitamin C essential for wound healing?
cards.back: Required for prolyl hydroxylase enzyme which hydroxylates proline and lysine in collagen synthesis

cards.front: What is the most common cause of impaired wound healing?
What is the difference between keloid and hypertrophic scar?
cards.back:
Keloid: Extends BEYOND wound margins, does NOT regress
Hypertrophic: Stays WITHIN wound margins, regresses with time

cards.front: Which areas are most prone to keloid formation?
cards.back: Earlobes, sternum, deltoid region, upper back

cards.front: What is the role of PDGF in wound healing?
cards.back: Released by platelets; causes chemotaxis and fibroblast proliferation

cards.front: What is the role of VEGF in wound healing?
Why do diabetic patients have impaired wound healing?
cards.back:
1. Microangiopathy (reduced blood supply)
2. Impaired immune function
3. Neuropathy (unrecognized injuries)

When can sutures typically be removed from facial wounds?
